POLS 15 – Section 5 Example Solution

Hunter C. Smith

2026-01-12

We won’t be going over any new code this week. Instead, this is a chance for you to practice the analytical tools we’ve learned so far in the course to prepare for next week’s midterm exam.

Choose a Dataset

  • wb.RData: Data from the World Bank for economic and environmental variables from 2005.
  • voters.RData: Data from a 2016 USC / LA Times Presidential Poll of voter demographics and attitudes toward presidential candidates.
  • nba.RData: Statistics for NBA teams from 2012-2016.

Setup

Clear your environment, set your working directory, and upload your dataset of choice.

rm(list = ls())
#setwd("/home/jovyan/UCSB_PS15/Section 5/")
load("voters.RData")

Explore Your Dataset

Identify your sample size, number of variables, and variable names.

nrow(usc4)
## [1] 1039
ncol(usc4)
## [1] 10
names(usc4)
##  [1] "hhincome"    "education"   "age"         "trump_vote"  "clint_vote" 
##  [6] "prob_vote"   "race"        "gender"      "statereside" "vote"

Before moving on, hypothesize a relationship between two variables in your dataset. Which is your IV, and which is your DV?

IV: Education (education)
DV: Probability of voting for Hillary Clinton (clint_vote)
Hypothesis: education is positively correlated with probability of voting for Clinton

Univariate Analysis

For each variable, compute the mean, median, range, and standard deviation. What do these values tell you about the distribution of each variable?

summary(usc4$education)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##    3.00   10.00   11.00   11.22   13.00   16.00
sd(usc4$education)
## [1] 2.168303
summary(usc4$clint_vote)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##     0.0     0.0    40.0    44.7   100.0   100.0
sd(usc4$clint_vote)
## [1] 45.25191

Education: Mean \(\approx\) median, so probably normally distributed. Small SD, so probably tight distribution.
Clinton Vote: Mean > median, so positively skewed. Large SD, so lots of variation in values (probably lots of extreme values).

Now, create a distribution plot for each variable (with appropriate labels). Describe each of the distributions.

hist(usc4$education,
     xlab = "Education",
     main = "Distribution of Education",
     col = "blue")

hist(usc4$clint_vote,
     xlab = "Prob. Clinton Vote",
     main = "Distribution of Prob. Clinton Vote",
     col = "red")

Both distributions are bimodal. Modal responses for clint_vote are at extreme values while modal responses for education are near the center of the distribution.

Bivariate Analysis

Compute the covariance and correlation between your variables and interpret each – what do they tell you about the relationship?

cov(usc4$education, usc4$clint_vote)
## [1] 16.54657
cor(usc4$education, usc4$clint_vote)
## [1] 0.1686363

Covariance: There is a positive association between education and clint_vote.
Correlation: There is a weak positive correlation between education and clint_vote.

Now make a scatterplot of these variables. Is there a clear pattern in the data?

plot(usc4$education, usc4$clint_vote,
     xlab = "Education",
     ylab = "Prob. Clinton Vote",
     main = "Scatterplot of Education and Prob. Clinton Vote")

Difficult to tell visually, but correlation tells us that there should be a slight upward trend.

Write the regression model you’ll estimate with these variables using appropriate mathematical notation.

Equation: \[clint\_vote_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 education_i + \epsilon_i\]

Now estimate your regression and add the line to your scatterplot. What does the line suggest about the relationship?

mod1 <- lm(clint_vote ~ education, data = usc4)

plot(usc4$education, usc4$clint_vote,
     xlab = "Education",
     ylab = "Prob. Clinton Vote",
     main = "Scatterplot of Education and Prob. Clinton Vote")
abline(mod1, col = "blue", lwd = 2)

Confirms that there is a positive relationship because the regression line has a positive slope.

Finally, pull up the model summary and do the following:

  • Interpret \(\hat{\beta_0}\).
  • Interpret \(\hat{\beta_1}\).
  • Specify your null hypothesis using both mathematical notation and words.
  • Specify your alternate hypothesis using both mathematical notation and words.
  • Interpret the \(p\)-value for \(\hat{\beta_1}\) and state your conclusions regarding significance and your hypotheses.
  • Interpret \(R^2\).
summary(mod1)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = clint_vote ~ education, data = usc4)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -61.51 -40.39 -10.95  48.55  80.72 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)   5.1976     7.3019   0.712    0.477    
## education     3.5194     0.6388   5.509 4.54e-08 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 44.63 on 1037 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.02844,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.0275 
## F-statistic: 30.35 on 1 and 1037 DF,  p-value: 4.541e-08

\(\hat{\beta_0}\): When a person has not received any education, their predicted probability of voting for Clinton is 5.19 percentage points.
\(\hat{\beta_1}\): A one-year increase in education is associated with a 3.52 percentage point increase in the probability of voting for Clinton.

\(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\). Education does not affect the probability of voting for Clinton.
\(H_A: \beta_1 \neq 0\). Education does affect the probability of voting for Clinton.

The \(p\)-value associated with \(\hat{\beta_1}\) is 0.0000000454. This is less than the standard significance threshold (\(\alpha = 0.05\)), so we have sufficient evidence to reject the hull hypothesis.

\(R^2\) = 0.02844, indicating that the specified model explains 2.84 percent of the variation we observe in the probability of voting for Clinton. Because this is a bivariate model, we can also say that accounting for education explains about 2.84 of the variation we observe in the probability of voting for Clinton.

Bonus – Heterogeneous Effects

If you have time, choose another variable from your dataset that might influence your results (think back to the example from Week 3 with the terror data). Create a meaningful subset of your data and compare the means of your variables. Does it seem like there may be heterogeneous effects?

mean(usc4$education[usc4$age >= mean(usc4$age)])
## [1] 11.21042
mean(usc4$education[usc4$age < mean(usc4$age)])
## [1] 11.23608
mean(usc4$clint_vote[usc4$age >= mean(usc4$age)])
## [1] 46.63127
mean(usc4$clint_vote[usc4$age < mean(usc4$age)])
## [1] 42.77351
usc4$age_bin <- ifelse(usc4$age >= mean(usc4$age), 1, 0) # creating a binary variable based on the age breakpoint used above
t.test(usc4$clint_vote ~ usc4$age_bin) # testing whether the difference in clint_vote based on age_bin is significant
## 
##  Welch Two Sample t-test
## 
## data:  usc4$clint_vote by usc4$age_bin
## t = -1.3744, df = 1035.5, p-value = 0.1696
## alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group 0 and group 1 is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -9.365486  1.649963
## sample estimates:
## mean in group 0 mean in group 1 
##        42.77351        46.63127

Very slight difference-in-means for education based on age, but larger for clint_vote. The \(p\)-value reported in the t-test demonstrates that this difference is not statistically significant, though, so there are likely not heterogeneous effects based on age.
